nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical and subtropical coastal waters; typically found on sandy and rubble substrates, reef flats, and seagrass-adjacent zones in the photic zone

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing. Sole hobby trade source for this species.

summary: Mexican Fern Caulerpa is a feathery green macroalgae native to the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean, with ornamental pinnate fronds growing from a creeping stolon. It grows quickly, takes well to beginner care, and — like all Caulerpa — releases compounds that can stress nearby corals. Sale is prohibited in California.
careNotes:
Mexican Fern Caulerpa (Caulerpa mexicana) is a siphonous green macroalgae — meaning its entire body is a single giant cell with no dividing walls — that produces fern-like pinnate fronds (flattened blades with small side-branches arranged like a feather) arising from a creeping stolon. The stolon anchors into sandbed or rockwork via root-like structures called rhizoids. The fronds are considered particularly ornamental for the genus, making Mexican Fern Caulerpa suitable for display placement as well as refugium use. Growth tends to be prolific — as is typical across the Caulerpa genus — and in the right conditions the colony may expand rapidly.

ALLELOPATHY: Like all Caulerpa species, C. mexicana is expected to release secondary metabolites — including caulerpin and caulerpenyne — into the water column. These compounds have been shown to inhibit or stress nearby corals and competing algae in research settings. Physical separation from sensitive SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals, which are among the more chemically sensitive reef invertebrates) is generally advised, and running activated carbon in the filtration system is a common mitigation. Refugium placement limits direct allelopathic exposure to display corals.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: As with all Caulerpa species, C. mexicana can undergo a process called holocarpy — a stress-triggered event in which the entire organism converts its cytoplasm to gametes and effectively dies, releasing large quantities of organic matter into the water column. Triggers include sharp nutrient crashes (running nitrate or phosphate to zero), a disrupted photoperiod, or rapid temperature swings. To reduce the risk: run refugium lighting on a continuous 24-hour cycle; harvest regularly to keep the colony in active vegetative growth; maintain stable (not zero) nutrient levels; and remove immediately any specimen showing white or translucent fronds or signs of turbid gamete release.

INVASIVE CONCERN: Mexican Fern Caulerpa is considered potentially invasive, consistent with the broader Caulerpa genus profile. Captive specimens must never be released into natural waterways. The species is banned for sale in California.

GENERAL CARE: High-intensity reef lighting (5,000K or higher; 8 or more hours per day for display use, or a continuous 24-hour cycle for refugium use) is generally recommended for Caulerpa species. Moderate water flow is broadly suitable; gentle tumbling suits refugium deployment. Freshly arrived specimens may appear white or translucent due to chloroplast displacement during dark shipping — this is normal, and coloration typically recovers within hours of light exposure. Herbivorous tankmates such as tangs, rabbitfish, and sea urchins will readily graze this species.

CROSS-REFERENCE: See feather-caulerpa (sw-algae-035, Caulerpa sertularioides) and mega-fern-caulerpa (sw-algae-057, Caulerpa ashmeadii) for related fern-form Caulerpa species with similar morphology and husbandry requirements.
breedingNotes: Mexican Fern Caulerpa propagates readily through fragmentation — detached frond pieces have been reported to establish as new colonies across the Caulerpa genus. Vegetative tip growth along the stolon is the primary mode of expansion in captivity.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole known hobby trade source at time of research. Confirms species identity (Caulerpa mexicana), fern-like morphology, prolific growth, display suitability, invasive designation, and California ban. Water parameters and detailed husbandry extrapolated from Caulerpa genus standards.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa (genus)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales, Class Ulvophyceae, Division Chlorophyta). Confirmed C. mexicana as a recognized species in the genus. General genus characteristics: siphonous unicellular structure, tropical distribution, photic zone (0–100 m), secondary metabolites (caulerpin, caulerpenyne), and invasive designations for the broader genus. Species-specific data for C. mexicana not available at the genus article level.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
